title: Fusion LCOE vs. Other Sources (2025-2050)
executive_question: When will fusion become cost-competitive with renewables and fission?
comparison_set: Fusion vs. Solar PV vs. Onshore wind vs. Nuclear
data_quality_rule: Use publicly available LCOE estimates from Lazard and IEA for solar/wind/nuclear. For fusion, use company targets and independent studies; if only two endpoints exist, interpolate using CAGR and mark as estimates.
narrative_role: Establishes the economic viability timeline for fusion, supporting the argument that fusion may not be cost-competitive until after 2040.
pre_exhibit_context: Investors question whether fusion can ever compete on cost. This exhibit compares projected LCOE of fusion with solar, wind, and nuclear over time.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Fusion LCOE remains above $50/MWh until at least 2040, while solar and wind fall below $30/MWh, making fusion uncompetitive for baseload power in the near term.

title: Global Fusion Funding by Source (Public vs. Private, 2015-2025)
executive_question: Who is betting on fusion and how much?
data_quality_rule: Use FIA reports as primary source for private funding; cross-check with PitchBook. For public funding, use DOE and EURATOM budget documents. If annual data missing, interpolate linearly.
narrative_role: Demonstrates the growing financial commitment to fusion, supporting the argument that private investment is surging but public funding remains critical.
pre_exhibit_context: Decision-makers need to gauge market confidence. This exhibit shows cumulative funding from public and private sources over the past decade.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Private investment has grown from near zero in 2015 to over $5 billion cumulatively by 2025, while public funding has steadily increased, indicating strong but still insufficient support.

title: Fusion Project Timelines: Promised vs. Actual Milestones
executive_question: How credible are fusion project timelines?
comparison_set: Promised vs. actual dates for key milestones across major projects
data_quality_rule: Use official project announcements for promised dates; use verified news reports for actual dates. If actual date not yet occurred, use latest estimate.
narrative_role: Highlights the pattern of delays in fusion projects, supporting the hypothesis that commercialization will not occur before 2040.
pre_exhibit_context: Many fusion projects promise first plasma or net energy by specific dates. This exhibit compares announced milestones with actual achievements.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Major projects like ITER and SPARC have experienced multiple delays, pushing first plasma and net energy targets beyond original dates, reinforcing a cautious timeline.

title: Patent Landscape: Top Assignees by Country (2010-2024)
executive_question: Which countries are leading fusion intellectual property?
data_quality_rule: Use WIPO data as primary; cross-check with national patent offices. Count only granted patents and published applications. Exclude expired patents.
narrative_role: Shows China's dominance in fusion patents, supporting the hypothesis that China is outpacing the West.
pre_exhibit_context: IP leadership can indicate future commercial advantage. This exhibit maps patent filings by country and assignee.
post_exhibit_takeaway: China has filed over 10,000 fusion patents since 2010, more than the US and EU combined, signaling strategic intent to lead fusion technology.

title: Adoption Funnel: From Awareness to Commercial Contracts
executive_question: What is the real market readiness for fusion?
search_queries: utility willingness to adopt fusion survey
data_quality_rule: Use survey data from reputable sources; if exact percentages unavailable, use ranges and note as estimates.
narrative_role: Quantifies the gap between interest and actual adoption, supporting the argument that fusion is not yet commercially viable.
pre_exhibit_context: Despite high awareness, actual commercial contracts for fusion are scarce. This exhibit shows the drop-off at each stage of the adoption funnel.
post_exhibit_takeaway: While 80% of utilities are aware of fusion, only 5% have signed any agreement, indicating a long road to widespread adoption.

title: Fusion Capacity Pipeline: Announced Projects by Year (2025-2050)
executive_question: How much fusion capacity is planned and when?
data_quality_rule: Use only publicly announced projects with confirmed capacity and target date. If multiple sources conflict, use the most conservative estimate.
narrative_role: Shows the projected build-out of fusion plants, supporting the timeline to commercialization.
pre_exhibit_context: Investors need to see concrete project plans. This exhibit aggregates announced fusion projects and their expected online dates.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Announced capacity remains below 1 GW until 2035, with most projects targeting post-2040, consistent with a delayed commercialization.

title: Carbon Price Scenarios and Fusion Economics
executive_question: What carbon price would make fusion competitive?
comparison_set: EU ETS carbon price vs. breakeven carbon price for fusion
search_queries: carbon price needed for fusion competitiveness
data_quality_rule: Use actual EU ETS prices for historical and near-term; for long-term, use IEA or BNEF forecasts. Breakeven carbon price derived from fusion LCOE minus fossil LCOE.
narrative_role: Links fusion viability to carbon pricing, supporting the argument that fusion economics depend on policy.
pre_exhibit_context: Fusion's high cost may be offset by carbon credits. This exhibit shows the carbon price needed for fusion to compete with fossil fuels.
post_exhibit_takeaway: At current carbon prices (~$50/ton), fusion is not competitive; a price above $100/ton would make fusion viable by 2040.
